title: AMD Instinct GPU Specifications: MI250X, MI300X, and MI325X for AI and HPC Workloads
text:
The AMD Instinct MI250X, released in 2021 and based on the CDNA2 architecture, is a dual-die accelerator featuring 128GB of HBM2e memory (64GB per GCD). It delivers a memory bandwidth of 3.28 TB/s. FP16 and BF16 performance both reach 383 TFLOPS, while FP32 and FP64 performance are each 47.9 TFLOPS, making the MI250X exceptionally strong for HPC and scientific computing workloads that require double-precision arithmetic. INT8 throughput is 383 TOPS. The TDP is 560W. Each GCD contains 14,080 stream processors, for a total of two GCDs per package. The MI250X is manufactured on a combination of 6nm TSMC and 7nm process nodes. AMD Infinity Fabric provides 200 GB/s of bandwidth between the two GCDs and 400 GB/s for multi-GPU communication. The MI250X is deployed in the Frontier supercomputer at Oak Ridge National Laboratory, which was the world's first exascale computing system. The energy efficiency of the MI250X is approximately 0.68 TFLOPS per watt at FP16.

The AMD Instinct MI300X, released in 2023 and based on the CDNA3 architecture, features 192GB of HBM3 memory, which is 2.4x more than the NVIDIA H100. Memory bandwidth reaches 5.3 TB/s, which is 1.6x more than the H100. FP16 and BF16 performance with sparsity reach 1,307.4 TFLOPS, while FP8 performance with sparsity reaches 2,614.9 TFLOPS. FP32 performance is 163.4 TFLOPS and FP64 performance is 81.7 TFLOPS. INT8 throughput with sparsity is 2,614.9 TOPS. The TDP is 750W. The MI300X contains 304 Compute Units and 19,456 stream processors. It is manufactured on the 5nm TSMC process and contains 153 billion transistors. The MI300X features 8 HBM3 stacks. AMD Infinity Fabric provides 896 GB/s of GPU-to-GPU bandwidth. The energy efficiency of the MI300X is approximately 0.87 TFLOPS per watt at FP16 dense. The MI300X is competitive with the NVIDIA H100 for LLM inference workloads due to its significantly larger memory capacity. The MI300X supports the ROCm software stack, which is an open-source alternative to NVIDIA's CUDA.

The AMD Instinct MI325X, released in 2024 as an enhanced version of the CDNA3 architecture, features 256GB of HBM3e memory, which is 33% more than the MI300X. Memory bandwidth reaches 6 TB/s. The MI325X shares the same compute architecture as the MI300X, delivering 1,307.4 TFLOPS of FP16 performance with sparsity and 2,614.9 TFLOPS of FP8 performance with sparsity. The TDP remains at 750W. The MI325X is enhanced specifically for large model inference workloads where memory capacity is the primary bottleneck. It offers better memory efficiency compared to the MI300X.

When comparing AMD Instinct accelerators to NVIDIA counterparts, the MI300X has 2.4x more memory than the H100 (192GB vs 80GB) and 1.6x more memory bandwidth, but the NVIDIA software ecosystem remains more mature. Compared to the H200, the MI300X has 36% more memory (192GB vs 141GB) with comparable bandwidth. The MI325X has 82% more memory than the H200 (256GB vs 141GB). Regarding software, CUDA has broader framework support than ROCm, but ROCm has improved significantly and now supports PyTorch and other major machine learning frameworks. The MI300X is often 10-20% cheaper per hour on cloud instances compared to the H100. For LLM inference, the MI300X can serve larger models without requiring model parallelism due to its 192GB memory capacity. For HPC workloads, AMD's FP64 performance is significantly better than that of NVIDIA consumer GPUs.

Regarding energy efficiency trends, the Frontier supercomputer at Oak Ridge National Laboratory uses 9,408 MI250X GPUs and achieves 1.1 exaFLOPS while consuming approximately 21 MW of power. The MI300X has a TDP of 750W but delivers higher throughput than the H100 (700W TDP) for memory-bound workloads. AMD's chiplet design enables incremental improvements in memory capacity without requiring a full chip redesign, improving manufacturing efficiency. For inference workloads, the MI300X and MI325X's larger memory capacity reduces the number of GPUs needed to serve large models, potentially reducing total system power consumption.
